data.title: United Community Banks signals 3-5 bps Q2 NIM expansion while outlining Peach State deal at 40% cost savings
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: United Community Banks (UCB) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We had a great start to 2026." (President, Chairman & CEO Herbert Harton) said the company delivered net income "of a little over $84 million" and "EPS of $0.69," adding that "on an operating basis, our EPS was $0.70." Harton tied performance to "annualized loan growth of 4.5%" and "an expansion of our net interest margin of 3 basis points," while also pointing to credit metrics including "total charge-offs of 22 basis points, only 10 basis points, excluding Navitas."

  • Harton highlighted capital actions, including "a $0.25 quarterly dividend" and "the repurchase of $37 million of our common stock," and added: "We also announced the intention to redeem our remaining $100 million in sub debt in the second quarter." He said tangible book value per share "grew at an annualized rate of nearly 6% for the quarter and by 10% year-over-year."

  • "On an end-of-period basis, our customer deposits grew by $237 million or 4% annualized, mostly driven by DDA growth in the quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Jefferson Harralson) also said "our cost of deposits moved down 9 basis points to 1.67%" and that "our cumulative total deposit beta stands at 39% in this down cycle."

  • On the Peach State Bank acquisition, Harralson said, "Peach State has approximately $800 million in assets" and "the deal value is about $100 million and will be a 50-50 cash stock mix." He added, "we are estimating 40% cost savings" and "we plan on repurchasing the $50 million in shares issued by year-end," with expected accretion: "$0.09 accretive in 2027" and "$0.12 accretive" with the planned buybacks.

Outlook

  • Management’s explicit forward-looking guidance in the prepared remarks centered on margin: "I would expect the margin to be up between 3 and 5 basis points in the second quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Harralson)

  • In Q&A, Bradshaw framed loan growth expectations: "we remain optimistic. We think it will be in the 5% to 6% range, providing nothing else goes on unusual in Iran." (President & Chief Banking Officer Bradshaw)

  • Harralson reiterated deposit-cost expectations: "I would expect our deposit cost to be relatively flat." (Executive VP & CFO Harralson)

Financial Results

  • "Our net interest margin increased 3 basis points in the quarter to 3.65% and up 29 basis points compared to last year," (Executive VP & CFO Harralson) who also stated "the first quarter is the fifth quarter in a row of margin expansion" and cited "$1.4 billion of assets, paying down in the 4.63% range" as part of the repricing tailwind.

  • Harralson reported fee income and notable items: "Noninterest income was $43.7 million in the quarter" and included "a $5.2 million gain on an interest rate cap" tied to debt the company "intend[s] to redeem on April 30." He also said, "Last quarter, we sold $41.6 million in Navitas loans compared to $8.3 million this quarter."

  • On expenses and one-time items, Harralson said, "Our GAAP expenses were $157.3 million in the first quarter, and our operating expenses were $151.6 million" and described a "$1.9 million" FDIC special assessment release as well as "another nonoperating charge" tied to a payroll timing change.

  • Credit provisioning and coverage were described as: "Our loan loss provision was $10.9 million in the quarter" and "our allowance coverage of credit losses moved down slightly to 1.15%." (Executive VP & CFO Harralson)

Q&A

  • Jake Morton, Stephens Inc.: asked whether deposit costs can fall further; Executive VP & CFO Harralson responded, "I would expect our deposit cost to be relatively flat" and said Peach State, "being only 3% of our assets, doesn't change those numbers meaningfully."

  • Michael Rose, Raymond James: asked if paydowns are easing and whether loan growth could accelerate; President & Chief Banking Officer Bradshaw said paydowns were largest in hospitality and added, "we remain optimistic" on "5% to 6%" loan growth, and described hiring momentum: "a net increase of 10 revenue producers" with a goal of "10% annual growth" in 2026.

  • Michael Rose, Raymond James: asked about expense control, hiring, and AI investments; Executive VP & CFO Harralson said expense growth is targeted at "this 3.5% range" and that hires "could add about $1 million to $1.2 million a quarter," while President, Chairman & CEO Harton said, "our fraud losses have actually dropped by 50% over the last 2 years" and that AI has enabled "take more calls with the same number of agents" and "more programming work today without adding programmers."

  • Gary Tenner, D.A. Davidson: asked if UCB could announce another deal; President, Chairman & CEO Harton said, "I don't believe in doing another deal while Peach State is active," but added he would move on "the right deal" with similar terms.

  • Stephen Scouten, Piper Sandler: asked about M&A size preferences and hiring pace; President, Chairman & CEO Harton said UCB typically targets "10%" to "15% or less" of its size, while President & Chief Banking Officer Bradshaw said, "we would be opportunistic" if the right bankers are available.

  • Kyle Gierman, Hovde Group: asked about mortgage sustainability and NPAs; Executive VP & CFO Harralson cited a rate dip that "helped promote a little mini refi boom" and noted Q2/Q3 seasonality strength, while Executive VP & Chief Risk Officer Robert Edwards said, "I sort of anticipate asset quality to be stable" and that NPA movement was "standard movement in and out of nonaccrual."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly pressing, concentrating on deposit-cost trajectory, the sustainability of loan growth, expenses (including hiring and AI), fee durability, and M&A cadence; questions repeatedly returned to deposit competition and the mechanics of buybacks tied to the Peach State transaction.

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and largely confident in Q&A, using firm expectations on margin and deposit costs ("I would expect our deposit cost to be relatively flat" and "I would expect the margin to be up between 3 and 5 basis points in the second quarter," Executive VP & CFO Harralson), while also acknowledging competitive pressure ("we are seeing competition out there," Executive VP & CFO Harralson).

  • Versus last quarter, the call leaned more toward execution and announced M&A specifics; management also provided a more explicit near-term margin expectation (Q2) than the prior call’s focus on Q1 repricing dynamics.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Strategic emphasis shifted from Q4’s description that "M&A opportunities are light" (Chairman & CEO Harton, prior call) to Q1’s announced Peach State acquisition with stated pricing, cost saves, and 2027 accretion targets.

  • NIM commentary advanced from Q4’s expectation that "the NIM [would] be up between 2 and 4 basis points in the first quarter" (Executive VP & CFO Harralson, prior call) to Q1’s explicit Q2 expectation of "between 3 and 5 basis points" (Executive VP & CFO Harralson).

  • Credit and funding metrics improved sequentially in the figures cited on the Q1 call: net charge-offs were stated at "22 basis points" in Q1 versus "34 basis points" in Q4, and deposit costs were stated at "1.67%" in Q1 versus "1.76%" in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Competition for deposits remained a recurring theme: "we are seeing competition out there" while also seeking growth in deposits. (Executive VP & CFO Harralson)

  • Mortgage and fee income sensitivity to rates was acknowledged: "The challenge in mortgage is interest rates drive so much of it." (President & Chief Banking Officer Bradshaw)

  • Credit discussion emphasized variability rather than deterioration: "I would expect NPAs to kind of fluctuate up and down." (Executive VP & Chief Risk Officer Edwards)

Final Takeaway

Management described a Q1 anchored by stated EPS of $0.69 ($0.70 operating), modest margin expansion to 3.65%, and continued capital return (dividend and $37 million of buybacks), alongside a planned $100 million subordinated debt redemption. The most material strategic update was the announced Peach State Bank acquisition, with management outlining a $100 million deal value, estimated 40% cost savings, and projected 2027 accretion of $0.09 (or $0.12 with planned buybacks), while also projecting 3-5 basis points of NIM expansion in Q2 and characterizing deposit costs as expected to remain relatively flat.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Trump says he’ll remember companies that don’t seek tariff refunds
data.description:
President Donald Trump said on Tuesday that he will “remember” U.S. companies that do not seek refunds for the tariffs he unilaterally imposed, which the Supreme Court ruled were illegal in a 6-3 decision.

“Brilliant if they don’t do that,” Trump told CNBC in an interview when asked about companies potentially avoiding refund requests to avoid offending him.

The comments came a day after U.S. Customs and Border Protection opened a portal for importers to seek more than $160B in potential refunds for the so-called IEEPA tariffs.

Major companies, including Apple (AAPL) and Amazon (AMZN) have not yet filed requests for refunds, prompting Trump to say he was “very honored” by the decision, adding, “If they don’t do that, they’ve got to know me very well.”

Trump also expressed frustration with the Supreme Court ruling, complaining that it did not include language stating, “You don’t have to pay back tariffs that have already been received.”

“So, I’m not happy with the Supreme Court, I’ll be honest with you,” he said.

While some tech giants have held back, major retailers crushed by the trade war are actively pursuing refunds.

Levi Strauss (LEVI) CFO Harmit Singh said the company expects around $80M in refunds, while Gap (GAP) CFO Katrina O’Connell said the company is “definitely working on gaining clarity on whether we can recognize a refund,” noting “the tariff impact has been significant to our performance.”

More on tariffs

U.S. Supreme Court strikes down Trump's global tariffs

U.S. prepares to refund billions from Trump tariffs, starting next week - report

Trump administration opens $166B tariff refund process after court loss

Costco and FedEx face lawsuits as tariff refund portal opens today

Trump tariffs could be reinstated by July, Treasury’s Bessent says

data.title: USA Rare Earth to seek more deals, growth to challenge China
data.description:
USA Rare Earth's (USAR) incoming president said she plans to grow the company through more acquisitions and new projects to challenge China and become the U.S. leader in rare earth production.

"There's optionality for both organic and inorganic growth at each step of the supply chain," Thras Moraitis, CEO of Brazil's Serra Verde Group and incoming president of USA Rare Earth (USAR), told Bloomberg after the companies announced a $2.8B deal on Monday.

The combined companies will keep seeking deals in the mining and magnet-making stages of the rare earth supply chain, although there are few opportunities to buy processing plants, USA Rare Earth (USAR) CEO Barbara Humpton said in the joint interview.

For processing, "it's going to have to be organic growth, because we simply don't have the metal-making capability outside of China," the CEO said. "We're going to have to grow it ourselves."

"We're just in the early innings for establishing a platform for growth," Humpton said.

The company completed an acquisition of Less Common Metals Ltd. in 2025, giving it possession of a metal and alloy manufacturer, and purchased a 12.5% stake in Carester SAS earlier this month in a bid to obtain the French company’s rare earth processing expertise, and it received $1.6B in proposed funding from the Commerce Department in January, which is conditional on reaching key milestones on the buildout of core projects.

data.title: Select industrials hit 52-wk highs as investors chase power, pricing, turnaround stories
data.description:
A cluster of industrial names touched fresh 52-week highs on Tuesday, suggesting investors are gravitating toward companies tied to several durable market themes: artificial intelligence infrastructure, electrification, utility spending and operational turnarounds.

Among the names making new highs were Caterpillar (CAT), Vertiv Holdings (VRT), Eaton (ETN), Pitney Bowes (PBI) and Custom Truck One Source (CTOS). While the businesses look very different on paper, the market appears to be rewarding exposure to capital spending cycles and improving earnings visibility.

Broader market indices slid into negative territory amid uncertainties in the Persian Gulf region.

Power demand becomes an industrial trade

One of the clearest themes is electricity. Vertiv (VRT), which provides cooling and power systems for data centers, has become a favored way to play the rapid buildout of AI infrastructure. As hyperscalers race to add computing capacity, investors have looked beyond chipmakers and into the physical equipment needed to keep servers running.

Eaton (ETN) has benefited from a similar trend, though with broader reach. The company’s electrical products business gives it exposure to grid upgrades, commercial construction, data centers and industrial automation. With utilities and large customers scrambling to modernize networks, Eaton (ETN) has become one of Wall Street’s preferred “picks and shovels” names for electrification.

Old economy, new demand

Caterpillar’s (CAT) move to a new high reflects continued confidence that heavy equipment demand can remain healthy even in a mixed economy. The company is tied to construction, mining, energy and infrastructure, all areas where spending has held up better than many expected. It also makes generators that have seen greater demand from AI data centers.

Some investors also view Caterpillar (CAT) as a hedge against a world where governments keep spending on roads, factories, energy projects and strategic domestic manufacturing.

Turnaround stories get rewarded, too

Not every winner was tied to megatrends. Pitney Bowes (PBI) surged after saying preliminary first-quarter profit would exceed prior expectations and after raising full-year guidance. The company projected adjusted earnings of about $0.47 per share, up from $0.33 a year earlier, while adjusted EBIT rose to about $130 million from $120 million.

That kind of update can be powerful in the current market, where investors have shown renewed appetite for companies demonstrating credible self-help progress rather than just macro exposure.

Niche utility buildout play emerges

Custom Truck One Source (CTOS) also joined the list after Cantor Fitzgerald initiated coverage with an Overweight rating. The firm said CTOS is well positioned for utility grid expansion, telecom projects and infrastructure demand.

The company rents and sells specialized trucks and equipment used in transmission work, which gives investors another way to play grid modernization without owning a utility directly.

What the group may be signaling

Taken together, the new highs suggest investors are rotating toward industrial companies with tangible earnings drivers rather than abstract promises. The favored formula appears to be straightforward: exposure to power demand, infrastructure spending, pricing strength or a management team finally getting its act together.

It is also a reminder that in 2026, some of the hottest momentum trades are not always software names. Sometimes they make transformers, excavators and mail machines.

Industrial names hitting 52-week highs

  • Caterpillar (CAT)
  • Custom Truck One Source (CTOS)
  • Eaton (ETN)
  • Pitney Bowes (PBI)
  • Vertiv Holdings (VRT)

data.title: Genuine Parts reaffirms 2026 adjusted EPS $7.50-$8.00 as separation targets Q1 2027 with $100M-$150M run-rate costs
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Genuine Parts Company (GPC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO William Stengel said, "the separation work is on track and progressing well" and added, "we're progressing well and on track with our timeline to complete the separation in the first quarter of 2027." He also stated, "during the first quarter, our teams did this well and delivered financial results ahead of our expectations."
  • On geopolitics, CEO Stengel cautioned, "the war in the Middle East will require us to remain agile and disciplined" and said, "the war is impacting the flow of certain goods across the global supply chain, adding inflationary pressure to certain product and logistics costs," while also noting, "we did not experience a material impact to our financial results during the first quarter."
  • CFO Herbert Nappier said, "our teams delivered in the first quarter with sales in line and profit ahead of expectations" and reported, "adjusted EPS of $1.77." He added that nonrecurring items tied to restructuring and the separation "totaled $75 million of pretax costs or $56 million after tax."

Outlook

  • CFO Nappier stated, "we are reaffirming our outlook for 2026" and guided, "diluted earnings per share... to be in the range of $6.10 to $6.60 and adjusted diluted earnings per share to be in the range of $7.50 to $8." He explained the unchanged stance: "we have balanced our performance to date against a more prudent view of the second and third quarters, given uncertainty of the conflict in Iran."
  • On sales, CFO Nappier reaffirmed, "we continue to expect total GPC sales growth in the range of 3% to 5.5%," and said the outlook assumes "market growth will be roughly flat" and pricing benefit "will be approximately 2%."
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call, management again kept the same full-year EPS range and total sales growth range, while adding more explicit commentary that the conflict-driven impact would be "most pronounced in Q2" (current call) versus prior-quarter emphasis on Europe and independent owners as the key swing factors.

Financial Results

  • The company reported total sales of $6.3B for the quarter, and CFO Nappier reported, "total GPC sales increased 6.8%," with comparable sales improvement of "240 basis points" and foreign currency benefit of "320 basis points."
  • CFO Nappier reported profitability and cost structure details: "our gross margin was 37.3%" and "our adjusted SG&A as a percentage of sales in the first quarter was 29.4%." He also said, "we incurred restructuring costs of $59 million and realized $26 million of cost savings or a benefit of $0.14 per share."
  • Cash and capital allocation items included: "we generated approximately $64 million in cash from operations," "we invested approximately $100 million... in the form of capital expenditures," and "we also returned approximately $142 million back to our shareholders in the form of dividends," according to CFO Nappier.

Q&A

  • Gregory Melich, Evercore ISI: asked whether pricing would run around recent levels as freight and supplier costs rise; CFO Nappier answered, "we expect the impact... to be most pronounced in Q2" and quantified that "we see some downside risk... of about $10 million to $20 million of EBITDA" as the net impact of the conflict, while adding, "we think the pricing environment stays more in line... 2%."
  • Bret Jordan, Jefferies: asked for more color on Europe and then dividend policy post-separation; CEO Stengel responded, "we saw sequential -- meaningful sequential improvement" and highlighted "some nice progress in our Germany business" and "really nice progress in our Iberia platform," while CFO Nappier said on capital allocation, "early innings" and reiterated, "we'll ensure that the capital allocation strategy of the 2 businesses follows its growth strategy" and intends "both companies investment-grade ratings."
  • Christopher Horvers, JPMorgan: asked about Section 232 steel tariffs and freight pass-through; CFO Nappier said, "we have not seen any material increases" on Section 232 and on freight/pricing added, "we'll be thinking about how do we pass that through" while emphasizing the conflict as "a near-term net negative."
  • Scot Ciccarelli, Truist: asked for more disclosure on company-owned vs independent profitability and then independent inventory appetite; CEO Stengel said, "I'm not sure we prefer to disclose that level of detail" but added, "we'll bring that to life in the Investor Day," and on independents stated, "the tone and the discussions are very positive and optimistic" and "we're also spending time thinking creatively about different ways to support their inventory investments."
  • Michael Lasser, UBS: asked whether supporting independents could trade off with free cash flow and how to model Q2; CFO Nappier said, "it doesn't impact the medium or long-term outlook for cash flow generation" and cautioned on FX, "we had 320 basis points of FX tailwind in Q1," adding, "keep the core kind of in line and don't model in a bunch of additional FX tailwind."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to skeptical, concentrating on conflict-related cost pressure, the durability of pricing, Europe, and independent-owner health, including questions probing pass-through mechanics and near-term profit risk.
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks ("delivered financial results ahead of our expectations") and more cautious/defensive in Q&A around uncertainty, stressing prudence and limited visibility, including "we've been pretty prudent and pragmatic" and "it's difficult to predict how the conflict will play out" (CFO Nappier).
  • Versus the prior quarter, the analyst tone shifted from Q4’s focus on Europe weakness and independent-owner softness to more urgent near-term questions about Iran-driven freight, oil volatility, and pricing; management added a tighter near-term frame, saying it "refine[d] our perspective to the next 100 days" (CFO Nappier).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Separation planning moved from announcement and early framing (prior quarter) to quantified run-rate cost ranges and more process detail (current quarter), with CEO Stengel now saying the estimate is "in the range of $100 million to $150 million" and that work is "on track" for Q1 2027 completion.
  • The macro emphasis changed: the prior quarter repeatedly described Europe as a "watchpoint" and downshift in independents; the current quarter continued to cite Europe and independents but elevated the Iran conflict as a driver of near-term cost and demand uncertainty, including a stated "$10 million to $20 million" EBITDA downside risk in Q2 (CFO Nappier).
  • Guidance posture was consistent quarter to quarter, with CFO Nappier reaffirming the same 2026 EPS ranges and sales growth framework described in the prior call, while adjusting the narrative toward a "more prudent view of the second and third quarters" (current call).

Risks and Concerns

  • CFO Nappier identified key swing factors for 2026 performance as "the market conditions in Europe, the performance of our independent owners in our U.S. NAPA business and the impact from the conflict in Iran, including the duration of any disruptions."
  • On conflict mechanics and mitigation, CFO Nappier said the company expects "near-term cost pressure" in Q2, aims to pass through many cost increases, and emphasized limited direct sourcing exposure: "our exposure to products sourced from the Middle East is less than 0.5% of our total purchases."
  • On separation cost risk, CFO Nappier described two cost buckets within the $100M-$150M range and noted, "this range of cost does not include onetime costs associated with the separation," adding, "we will share additional details" on corporate cost allocation later.

Final Takeaway

GPC’s management emphasized that the Automotive/Industrial separation remains on track for completion in Q1 2027, with estimated run-rate dis-synergies and stand-alone costs held at $100M-$150M, while reaffirming full-year 2026 guidance even after Q1 results that CFO Nappier said were ahead of expectations. In Q&A, executives repeatedly framed the Iran conflict as the key near-term variable, including a Q2 net EBITDA downside risk of $10M-$20M, alongside continued monitoring of Europe and U.S. independent-owner performance.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Equinix is the most overbought real estate stock as Q1 earnings roll on
data.description:
As the market moves into the second week of Q1 earnings, we look at the most overbought stocks in the real estate sector.

Below is a list of the top 10 real estate stocks ranked based on their Relative Strength Index. The list encompasses various sub-sectors within real estate, including Data Center REITs, Industrial REITs, and Real Estate Services.

The list is topped by Equinix (EQIX) and Digital Realty Trust (DLR), both sharing the highest RSI of 83. Jones Lang LaSalle (JLL) follows closely with an RSI of 78 and holds a “Strong Buy” quant rating.

Iron Mountain (IRM) ranks fourth with an RSI of 77, while retail-focused REITs like Brixmor Property Group (BRX) and Regency Centers (REG) also appear in the top ten. Industrial players such as EastGroup Properties (EGP), First Industrial Realty Trust (FR), and Terreno Realty (TRNO) round out the list.

The Relative Strength Index is a momentum oscillator that measures the velocity and magnitude of price changes, plotting them on a scale from 0 to 100. RSI compares the magnitude of recent gains to recent losses over a chosen lookback period—typically 14 days.

Here is the list:

  1. Equinix, Inc. (EQIX), RSI: 83

  2. Digital Realty Trust, Inc. (DLR), RSI: 83

  3. Jones Lang LaSalle Incorporated (JLL), RSI: 78

  4. Iron Mountain Incorporated (IRM), RSI: 77

  5. Brixmor Property Group Inc. (BRX), RSI: 76

  6. EastGroup Properties, Inc. (EGP), RSI: 76

  7. Regency Centers Corporation (REG), RSI: 76

  8. National Storage Affiliates Trust (NSA), RSI: 76

  9. First Industrial Realty Trust, Inc. (FR), RSI: 74

  10. Terreno Realty Corporation (TRNO), RSI: 74

Real Estate ETFs: (VNQ), (XLRE), (IYR), (USRT), and (HOMZ)

data.title: Valmont projects 2026 EPS of $21.50 to $23.50 as North America Utility demand stays elevated
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Valmont Industries (VMI) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Avner Applbaum framed Q1 as “a strong start to the year with sales growth, record first quarter earnings per share and progress against our strategic priorities,” adding, “Our strategy is anchored in markets with durable demand drivers, most notably utility while continuing to improve the quality and resiliency of our earnings.”

  • Applbaum highlighted momentum in Infrastructure tied to grid spending and data center load, saying “Our capacity expansion plans are on track,” and linking operational progress to “the 27% sales growth in North America Utility.”

  • On Agriculture, Applbaum said North America grew year-over-year “due to favorable pricing,” while noting Middle East disruption: “At the onset of the conflict, our Dubai facility operated at a minimal level… The plant has currently paused operations until conditions stabilize.” He said Valmont “mitigated some of this impact through our global manufacturing footprint.”

  • CFO John Schwietz reported, “Net sales of $1.03 billion increased 6.2% year-over-year,” and added, “Diluted earnings per share was $5.51, a 27.5% increase from prior year.”

  • Schwietz also flagged a disclosure change: “We have realigned to better reflect the markets that we serve and how we manage them,” including separate North America Infrastructure reporting and consolidating “international infrastructure and global solar into 1 product line.”

Outlook

  • Management raised full-year EPS guidance: Schwietz said, “Diluted earnings per share are projected to be in the range of $21.50 to $23.50,” and “We are increasing our full year EPS guidance.”

  • Valmont reiterated full-year sales range and shifted segment expectations: “Net sales are projected to be between $4.2 billion to $4.4 billion,” while “increasing infrastructure sales to be between $3.3 billion to $3.45 billion,” offset by Agriculture “between $0.9 billion to $0.95 billion.”

  • Tariffs were explicitly embedded in guidance. Schwietz said, “Also included in our EPS guidance is the impact of the tariff changes that went into effect on April 6,” adding the changes “primarily affect a portion of our North America Utility production sourced from Mexico,” and that Valmont is limiting impact by using “primary U.S melt and poured steel which limits the incremental Section 232 tariff to 10%.”

  • Compared with the prior quarter call, the company increased the low end of EPS guidance (previously $20.50 to $23.50) and raised Infrastructure sales expectations, while describing additional Agriculture selectivity “primarily related to the Middle East conflict.”

Financial Results

  • Schwietz detailed profitability expansion: “Operating income increased to $155.6 million and operating margins improved 190 basis points to 15.1%,” with “tax rate… approximately 26%.”

  • In Infrastructure, Schwietz reported “Sales of $806 million grew 14.1% year-over-year,” including “North America Utility sales increased 27.4% driven by pricing and higher volumes,” while “North America Lighting and Transportation declined 4.4% due to the production challenges.” He said Infrastructure operating income was “$143 million or 17.8% of net sales.”

  • In Agriculture, Schwietz said “sales decreased 15.1% year-over-year to $227 million,” but “operating margin improved to 14.8%… returning to double-digit levels,” citing “pricing, cost management, and risk mitigation.” He added, “we reached a settlement on the material Brazil legal matter… resolved within our existing accrual.”

  • Cash deployment included capacity CapEx and shareholder returns: Schwietz said operating cash flow was “$103.5 million,” CapEx “$35 million,” and “We returned $71 million to shareholders.” He also noted, “we… increased our quarterly dividend by 13% to $0.77 per share.”

Q&A

  • Nathan Jones, Stifel: Asked whether new Section 232 tariffs were more impactful than Valmont indicated; CFO Schwietz said the April 6 rules “are incorporated in our guidance,” and reiterated the goal “to be tariff-cost-profit neutral,” including pricing and supply-chain actions.

  • Nathan Jones, Stifel: Pressed on Utility capacity and productivity; CEO Applbaum said CapEx is “one lever,” but throughput is also being lifted via “innovation, continuous improvement,” hiring to address bottlenecks, and “Kaizen events,” adding, “We should see this trend continued into Q2.”

  • Christopher Moore, CJS Securities: Asked about backlog direction; CEO Applbaum said backlog is “relatively flat” sequentially and “up year-over-year,” and cited “best lead times in the industry right now between 42 to 44 weeks.”

  • Tomohiko Sano, JPMorgan: Asked about sustaining pricing power; CEO Applbaum said Valmont focuses on “value pricing,” called Valmont “the leader in the market with the highest market share,” and said “the entire industry has been very disciplined around pricing.”

  • Brian Drab, William Blair: Sought quantification of Mexico tariff cost; CEO Applbaum said “10% is part of the new regulation,” and CFO Schwietz added, “your general range, how you're thinking about it is approximately right.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: Slightly negative to pressing on tariffs and quantification, with repeated probing on Section 232 impact and mitigation timing.

  • Management: Slightly positive in prepared remarks and confident in Utility; more measured in Q&A on tariffs, emphasizing policy “clarity,” mitigation actions, and neutrality goals.

  • Versus last quarter, Q&A focus shifted from Brazil legal/credit cleanup and general Utility demand to tariff mechanics and near-term Utility capacity/throughput, while management maintained a similar confidence level on Utility demand.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Leadership and reporting changes stood out: Q1 introduced Schwietz as CFO and implemented a new Infrastructure revenue reporting realignment; the prior quarter featured former CFO Thomas Liguori and highlighted unusual Brazil legal reserves and credit losses.

  • Guidance language moved more explicitly to tariffs and Middle East conflict impacts in Agriculture in Q1, while Q4 emphasized confidence in achieving midpoint guidance and cited evolving tariff regulation risk.

  • Utility framing strengthened in Q1 with Applbaum calling demand “unprecedented,” and discussing lead times and pipeline dynamics, versus Q4’s emphasis on backlog and capacity coming online.

Risks and Concerns

  • Tariff exposure and mitigation timing remained a key risk theme; Schwietz said supply-chain and pricing actions “take a little bit of time to take hold,” while reiterating neutrality objectives.

  • Middle East operational disruption was explicit: Applbaum said the Dubai facility “paused operations until conditions stabilize,” and management noted logistics constraints and reduced operating capacity impacting execution pace.

  • Agriculture demand pressure signals included “no meaningful acceleration in the spring selling season,” and Brazil headwinds from “tight credit availability and delays in government-backed financing.”

Final Takeaway

Valmont’s management presented Q1 as driven by strong North America Utility demand and operational throughput gains, alongside a raised 2026 EPS range of $21.50 to $23.50. The call emphasized that April 6 tariff changes were incorporated into guidance with mitigation centered on U.S. melt-and-pour steel, while Agriculture outlook became more selective due to Middle East conflict impacts, including a paused Dubai facility, even as Q1 Agriculture margins returned to “double-digit levels.”

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Custom Truck One Source rated Overweight at Cantor Fitzgerald; stock hits 52-week high
data.description:
Cantor Fitzgerald on Tuesday initiated coverage of Custom Truck One Source (CTOS) with an Overweight rating and an $11 price target, saying the specialty equipment provider is positioned to benefit from utility grid upgrades, infrastructure spending and improving cash flow.

Analysts led by Manish Somaiya said CTOS operates in favorable end markets tied to power generation, grid modernization, telecom and rail, while also offering customers a vertically integrated model that spans manufacturing, rentals, used equipment sales and aftermarket service.

Cantor argued that structure gives CTOS a “one-stop-shop” advantage and should help the company gain market share over time.

Utility demand a key driver

Unlike general equipment rental peers that are more exposed to traditional construction cycles, CTOS (CTOS) is more closely tied to utility and infrastructure spending, according to the report.

Cantor said demand should be supported by transmission expansion, electrification, data-center power needs and broader grid modernization efforts. The analysts added that CTOS benefits from offering a wider range of products than many utility-focused rivals.

That combination of specialty rental focus, manufacturing capability and lifecycle monetization could support stronger long-term earnings and free cash flow than current valuation levels imply, the firm said.

Operational recovery gains traction

Cantor said CTOS (CTOS) emerged from a difficult 2024 with improving momentum last year. The firm noted that 2025 revenue reached a record $1.94 billion while adjusted EBITDA rose to $383.6 million, helped by stronger rental demand and higher equipment utilization.

Fourth-quarter fleet utilization reached 83.6%, the highest level in nearly three years, while average original equipment cost on rent increased 13.7% year over year. Cantor said those figures suggest the rental business has moved out of its 2024 downturn.

The report also cited stronger bid activity tied to late-2023 transmission opportunities that had not yet translated into project starts.

2026 seen as turning point

Cantor said 2026 could mark a pivotal shift from operational recovery to free cash flow generation and debt reduction. Management is projected to generate $410 million to $435 million in adjusted ebitda, with levered free cash flow of $50 million and net leverage falling below 4 times by year-end.

The firm said key drivers include lower net rental fleet investment, continued inventory normalization and better cash conversion from a younger fleet with higher rental utilization.

New reporting structure could unlock value

Beginning in 2026, CTOS plans to report separate results for its Equipment Rental Solutions and Sales segments, and for Truck Equipment & Manufacturing. Cantor said the added disclosure should improve transparency and make the investment story easier to understand.

The analysts also said the segmentation could create cleaner reporting lines for two distinct businesses, potentially increasing strategic flexibility or future monetization options.

Sum-of-the-parts suggests upside

Cantor valued CTOS using a sum-of-the-parts framework, applying an 8.5x EV/EBITDA multiple to the rental business and 9.5x to manufacturing, leading to its $11 price target.

The firm said the shares trade at a discount to peers despite improving utilization, lower capex needs and a stronger free cash flow outlook. Continued execution on rentals and debt reduction could narrow that gap, Cantor said.

data.title: Flexsteel anticipates Q4 sales relatively flat with operating margins similar to Q3 amid rising energy and foam input pressures
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Flexsteel Industries (FLXS) Q3 fiscal 2026

Management view

  • CEO Derek Schmidt said the quarter played out amid “increasingly uncertain and dynamic” conditions, citing “severe weather early in the quarter” and “heightened macroeconomic uncertainty stemming from the conflict in the Middle East,” which he said impacted consumer confidence, financial-market volatility, and energy costs.
  • CEO Schmidt highlighted relatively stable demand and profitability versus the prior-year period despite order volatility: “we delivered relatively stable year-over-year sales performance in the quarter and maintained solid operating margins of approximately 7%,” while noting “orders were down approximately 2.4% in the quarter” as retail partners managed inventories cautiously.
  • CEO Schmidt flagged escalating cost and supply risks that could affect near-term results, including higher fuel/energy costs that “are expected to expand the ocean freight and product costs later in the fourth quarter and into the first quarter of fiscal year 2027,” and a foam input disruption: “a fire last month at a large chemical factory in Texas… is hindering production of polyol… which could lead to product shortages and extended manufacturing lead times… as soon as May.”
  • CFO Michael Ressler reported top-line and backlog details and attributed the net sales change largely to pricing: “net sales were $115.1 million… The increase was primarily driven by pricing from tariff surcharges, offset by lower unit volume,” and said backlog ended at “$79.5 million,” “down approximately 3.5% from second quarter.”

Outlook

  • Management resumed a clearer near-term view versus last quarter’s pause on guidance, with CEO Schmidt stating: “we currently anticipate fourth quarter sales to be relatively flat with prior year levels and operating margins similar to third quarter performance.”
  • CEO Schmidt said mitigation actions are under evaluation but constrained by demand sensitivity: “As we consider potential actions to mitigate these impacts, including pricing and cost initiatives, we are being thoughtful given the current sensitivity of the consumer and the broader demand environment.”
  • CFO Ressler outlined inventory expectations tied to product launches: “We would expect inventory to probably grow here in the quarter modestly… several new product collections… are starting to flow and we hope to get those things in stock so we can start fulfilling orders.”

Financial results

  • CFO Ressler reported profitability and the key year-over-year distortion from the prior-year period: “GAAP operating income of $8.2 million or 7.1% of sales… compared to an operating loss of $5.1 million,” adding the prior-year loss “included a $14.1 million impairment charge on the right-of-use asset associated with our Mexicali lease.”
  • CFO Ressler described margin drivers as investment-led and referenced ongoing tariff mitigation: “Current quarter operating margin decreased 20 basis points… primarily driven by higher SG&A investments in consumer insights, innovation, demand generation and customer experience,” while adding, “The impact of tariffs… was mitigated through… cost savings initiatives, operational efficiencies and pricing actions.”
  • CFO Ressler detailed liquidity and working capital, emphasizing debt-free positioning and inventory normalization: “cash balance of $57.3 million… no bank debt,” and “Cash flow from operations… was $22.1 million, primarily due to a $14.5 million reduction in inventory.”

Q&A

  • Anthony Lebiedzinski, Sidoti & Company, LLC asked whether management could quantify weather’s impact on sales; CFO Ressler replied, “it’s really hard to put a specific number on it,” but said retailers reported “a meaningful impact,” and CEO Schmidt added March demand was “difficult to really determine whether it’s something more structural longer term or temporary,” citing “more cautious consumer behavior and more conservative inventory management.”
  • Lebiedzinski asked for pricing vs. unit-volume color; CFO Ressler said tariff pricing was “meaningful” and that “around 11% of our sales composition is from pricing,” while noting unit volume increases in “strategic accounts and health and wellness.”
  • Lebiedzinski pressed on mix and sustainability; CFO Ressler said new product sales were “somewhere in that 40% to 45% range,” and CEO Schmidt linked higher margins to “differentiated innovation that clearly meets an underserved or unmet consumer need.”
  • Lebiedzinski asked if disruptions could enable share gains; CEO Schmidt responded, “we’re in a position of strength… profitable… continue our growth investments,” adding this “should enable us to continue to gain share,” particularly if weaker competitors “have to pull back on investments.”
  • Lebiedzinski asked about inventory and cash deployment; CFO Ressler said inventory was normalized after buying ahead of tariffs and reiterated capital priorities: “maintain a strong balance sheet,” reinvest for growth, and “returning excess cash to shareholders through dividends and buybacks.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly positive but probing on demand durability and drivers, praising profitability while repeatedly pushing for quantification and durability (e.g., “try to put a number… impact of the severe weather,” and “do you expect this to continue?”).
  • Management sentiment was slightly cautious in prepared remarks and more guarded in Q&A on visibility, with CEO Schmidt saying, “visibility is pretty limited,” while still expressing confidence in positioning: “we are navigating this period from a position of strength.”
  • Versus last quarter, management’s tone shifted from “pause on providing any forward-looking guidance” (CFO Ressler, Q2) to offering a defined Q4 sales and margin view in Q3, while the risk framing broadened from tariffs to geopolitical energy costs and foam supply disruption.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q3 emphasized a “meaningful shift in demand patterns” tied to severe weather and Middle East-driven uncertainty, while Q2 framed conditions as “highly dynamic” with tariffs and uneven demand as primary variables.
  • Q3 introduced specific incremental operational risks not highlighted in Q2, including potential polyol allocation and “product shortages,” and a timeline for freight/product cost escalation “later in the fourth quarter and into the first quarter of fiscal year 2027.”
  • Q2 management avoided formal guidance (“pause on providing any forward-looking guidance”), while Q3 management provided an explicit expectation for Q4 sales and operating margin.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on pricing vs. unit volume, new products, and tariff impacts; Q3 questions expanded into weather-driven demand attribution and capital allocation tied to elevated cash.

Risks and concerns

  • CEO Schmidt cited demand risk from macro uncertainty: “Demand is likely to remain uneven,” and retail partners are “managing inventory levels closely.”
  • CEO Schmidt identified cost inflation risk from energy markets, stating pressures are impacting “domestic transportation costs immediately” and later “ocean freight and product costs.”
  • CEO Schmidt raised supply chain risk from the Texas chemical fire affecting polyol, warning it “could lead to product shortages and extended manufacturing lead times… as soon as May.”
  • CEO Schmidt flagged policy risk: “the tariff environment remains highly fluid and uncertain,” including potential new tariffs, interaction with Section 232, and “uncertainty around future trade negotiations, including USMCA,” that could affect Mexico sourcing.

Final takeaway

Management characterized Q3 as a quarter of stable sales and roughly 7% operating margins despite choppy orders and a late-quarter pullback, and it laid out an expectation for Q4 sales to be relatively flat year over year with operating margins similar to Q3. At the same time, management highlighted near-term risks that could affect demand and costs—energy-driven freight and product cost pressure, a potential foam input shortage from polyol allocation, and fluid tariff and trade policy—while reiterating plans to balance pricing and cost actions with consumer sensitivity, sustain investment in innovation and customer experience, and maintain a strong balance sheet with optionality for reinvestment and shareholder returns.
